coredata.dc:title: Biodegradable CoSnO3 nanozymes modulate pH-responsive graphene quantum dot release for synergistic chemo-sonodynamic-nanocatalytic cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jinyan
He, Xialing
Cai, Jinming
Zhang, Zhenlin
Bai, Xue
Zhang, Shan
Geng, Bijiang
Pan, Dengyu
Shen, Longxiang
coredata.dc:description: Albeit nanozymes are widely applied for nanocatalytic therapy (NCT), the unsatisfactory catalytic activity of nanozymes accompanied by complex tumor microenvironment (TME) greatly pose a barrier to the therapeutic effect of NCT. Moreover, developing a high-efficiency nanozyme with biodegradability is still a huge challenge, which hinders their further clinical translation. Herein, pH-responsive biodegradable CoSnO3 nanocubes are synthesized and employed as sonosensitizers and nanozymes due to their narrow bandgaps (1.6 eV) and Co2+/Sn4+-mediated multi-enzyme activities. After depositing the graphene quantum dot (GQD) topoisomerase inhibitors on CoSnO3 nanocubes, the heterojunction-fabricated GQD/CoSnO3 shows cascaded amplification of ROS generation, which is ascribed to the inhibition of electron-hole pair recombination, Co2+-mediated peroxidase (POD)-mimic catalytic reaction to generate OH, Co3+/Sn4+-mediated depletion of overexpressed GSH, and catalytic decomposition of endogenous H2O2 for providing more O2 for enhanced SDT. More importantly, GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions are slowly degraded in the weakly acidic TME, resulting the tumor-specific release of GQDs after completion of SDT, which could induce DNA damage and cell apoptosis for chemotherapy to further enhanced the antitumor efficacy. The synergetic therapeutic efficacy of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions through chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT could realize “three-in-one” multimodal oncotherapy to completely eliminate tumors without recurrence. This work provides a paradigm of exploring pH-responsive biodegradable nanozymes as a drug carrier to realize tumor-specific drug release for enhanced SDT and NCT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions. (a) Diagram showing the synthesis process of GQDs from julolidine through a microwave reaction. (b-f) TEM and HRTEM images of GQD, CoSnO3, and GQD/CoSnO3. (g) Absorption spectra of GQD, CoSnO3, and GQD/CoSnO3. (h-m) Survey XPS (h), high-resolution Co 2p (i), Sn 3d (j), C 1 s (k), N 1 s (l), and O 1 s (m) spectra of GQD/CoSnO3. (n) XRD patterns of CoSn(OH)6, CoSnO3, GQD, and GQD/CoSnO3. (o) Zeta potential of GQD, CoSnO3, and GQD/CoSnO3.

Enhanced sonodynamic properties of GQD/CoSnO3. (a-c) US-activated 1O2 generation evaluation of GQD/CoSnO3 (a), CoSnO3 (b), and GQD (c). (d) Comparison of the rate constant of 1O2 generated by GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3 and GQD. (e) ESR spectra of 1O2 generated by GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, and GQD. (f-h) Tauc plot and XPS-VB spectra of CoSnO3 and GQD. (i) Schematic illustration of the bandgap structure and charge transfer process within the Z-scheme heterojunction.

Heterojunction-enhanced triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities of GQD/CoSnO3. (a-c) The change of TMB absorption spectra in the presence of GQD/CoSnO3 (a), CoSnO3 (b), or GQD (c) with the addition of H2O2 at pH 4.5. (d) Comparison of the POD-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3. (e, f) The change of TMB absorption spectra in the presence of GQD/CoSnO3 (e) or CoSnO3 (f) with the addition of H2O2 at pH 6.5. (g, h) Comparison of the POD-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3 (g) or CoSnO3 (h) at pH 4.5 or 6.5. (i, l) Comparison of the CAT-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, or GQD. (j, k) O2 generation efficiency of GQD/CoSnO3 or CoSnO3 at pH 4.5 in the presence of H2O2. (m-p) GSH-px-mimic catalytic activity evaluation of GQD/CoSnO3 (m), CoSnO3 (n), and GQD (o).

pH-responsive GQD release behavior of GQD/CoSnO3. (a, b) UV–vis absorption (a) and fluorescence (b) spectra of free GQDs in the supernatant at different times (0, 1, 2, 3, and 7 d). (c) GQD release from GQD/CoSnO3 in buffer solution (pH 4.5, 6.5 or 7.4) at different times (0, 1, 2, 3, 5, and 7 d). (d) TEM images of GQD/CoSnO3 after dialysis of 7 days at pH 7.4. (e-h) TEM images of GQD/CoSnO3 in buffer solution (pH 6.5) at different times (0, 1, 3, and 7 d). (i-k) 1O2 generation ability evaluation of GQD/CoSnO3 in buffer solution (pH 6.5) at different times (1, 3, and 7 d) under US irradiation. (l) Rate constant comparison of 1O2 generation ability of GQD/CoSnO3 in buffer solution (pH 6.5) at different times (1, 3, and 7 d) under US irradiation. (m) Confocal images of Hela cells incubated with GQD/CoSnO3 for different times (0, 1, 2, 3, 5, and 7 d).

In vitro antitumor effects of GQD/CoSnO3. (a-d) Cytotoxicity of GQD/CoSnO3 against 143B cells after incubation of 24, 48, 72, or 96 h in the absence or presence of US irradiation. (e, f) Live/dead cell and ROS staining images of 143B cells after different treatments.

In vivo antitumor effects of GQD/CoSnO3. (a) Illustration of the treatment schedule on 143B-tumor-bearing mice via GQD/CoSnO3. (b–e) In vivo and Ex vivo NIR imaging of mice after intravenous injection of ICG-labeled GQD/CoSnO3. (f) Biodistribution measurements of GQD/CoSnO3 in mice at 24 h post-injection. (g-k) Tumor growth (g), survival (h), body weight curves (i), H&E (j), and TUNEL (k) staining after different treatments.

Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure and mechanisms of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions for synergistic chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT through the enhanced sonodynamic performance and triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activity as well as the pH-responsive GQD release.

head.ce:title: Biodegradable CoSnO3 nanozymes modulate pH-responsive graphene quantum dot release for synergistic chemo-sonodynamic-nanocatalytic cancer therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Albeit nanozymes are widely applied for nanocatalytic therapy (NCT), the unsatisfactory catalytic activity of nanozymes accompanied by complex tumor microenvironment (TME) greatly pose a barrier to the therapeutic effect of NCT. Moreover, developing a high-efficiency nanozyme with biodegradability is still a huge challenge, which hinders their further clinical translation. Herein, pH-responsive biodegradable CoSnO3 nanocubes are synthesized and employed as sonosensitizers and nanozymes due to their narrow bandgaps (1.6 eV) and Co2+/Sn4+-mediated multi-enzyme activities. After depositing the graphene quantum dot (GQD) topoisomerase inhibitors on CoSnO3 nanocubes, the heterojunction-fabricated GQD/CoSnO3 shows cascaded amplification of ROS generation, which is ascribed to the inhibition of electron-hole pair recombination, Co2+-mediated peroxidase (POD)-mimic catalytic reaction to generate OH, Co3+/Sn4+-mediated depletion of overexpressed GSH, and catalytic decomposition of endogenous H2O2 for providing more O2 for enhanced SDT. More importantly, GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions are slowly degraded in the weakly acidic TME, resulting the tumor-specific release of GQDs after completion of SDT, which could induce DNA damage and cell apoptosis for chemotherapy to further enhanced the antitumor efficacy. The synergetic therapeutic efficacy of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions through chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT could realize “three-in-one” multimodal oncotherapy to completely eliminate tumors without recurrence. This work provides a paradigm of exploring pH-responsive biodegradable nanozymes as a drug carrier to realize tumor-specific drug release for enhanced SDT and NCT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Current clinical cancer therapy modalities, such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, are inadequate to eradicate malignant tumor due to the severe adverse effects and remarkable systematic toxicity [1–3]. Over the past decades, the aggressive drug resistance, poor accumulation in tumor tissue, low water solubility, severe off-target toxicity, and rapid clearance of many conventional chemotherapeutic drugs commonly result in unsatisfactory anticancer effect and impair their clinical applications [4,5]. These issues promote many researchers to develop drug delivery systems based on nanomaterials for increasing drug solubility, circulation time, anticancer efficiency, as well as reducing the in vivo long-term toxicity [6,7]. To achieve these goals, developing tumor microenvironment (TME)-responsive nanoplatform or modifying drugs with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and active targeting ligands has been regarded as a promising strategy that could obviously improve selective drug release and therapeutic outcome [8–10]. However, the extended circulation lifetime achieved by PEGylation often leads to the inefficient cellular uptake and decreased penetration capabilities of drugs. In addition, slow drug release, multiple cellular barriers, and intrinsic resistance mechanism still restrain the bioavailability and effectiveness of anticancer drugs. Therefore, it is imperative to explore multifunctional nanomaterials with distinct physicochemical properties to bypass these barriers during the stimulate-responsive drug delivery process.

Apart from developing stimulate-responsive drug delivery system, another strategy to improve the therapeutic efficiency is to combine chemotheray with other therapeutic modalities [11–13]. Recently, the amplification of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) level triggered by photodynamic therapy (PDT) [14–17], nanocatalytic therapy (NCT) [18–22], or sonodynamic therapy (SDT) [23–26] is being intensely explored to treat various cancer and attracted widespread research interest. Based on the enzymatic reactions in TME, various nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities can combine the advantages of nanomaterials and natural enzymes, catalyzing the generation of highly cytotoxic ROS and achieving satisfactory NCT [27,28]. Unlike low-efficiency and unstable natural enzymes, several features of tunable activity, large-scale production, and high reproducibility make nanozymes possess enhanced catalytic activity [29–31]. However, most of the reported nanozymes including noble metal nanoparticles, metal oxide or sulfide, and 2D nanomaterials, still suffer from the low catalytic activity and single-enzyme mimetic activity, thereby severely restraining their tumor therapeutic efficacy.

Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) with sonosensitizers enables the precise tumor treatment owing to its capability for selective ultrasound (US) irradiation and generating ROS for killing cancer cells with minimal side effects [32–36]. In comparison to the limited penetration depth of PDT mediated by NIR laser even in the NIR-II window (<1 cm), SDT triggered by ultrasound (US) possesses larger penetration depth (>10 cm), higher therapeutic efficiency, and lower side effects, showing great promise for treating deep-seated tumors [37–41]. However, the unsatisfactory sonodynamic efficiency of sonosensitizers and adverse TME restriction severely restrict the therapeutic efficacy of SDT since sonosensitizers would absorb US energy and transfer energy to surrounding molecules including O2 and H2O for producing highly cytotoxic ROS such as 1O2 and OH [42–44]. The low water solubility, limited stability, poor biocompatibility of organic sonosensitizers and the wide bandgap, fast e−–h+ pair recombination, non-degradability of inorganic sonosensitizers represented by TiO2 greatly limit their SDT applications [45–47]. In addition, insufficient O2 supply in TME would reduce the therapeutic efficacy of SDT [17,18,48–50]. Increasing efforts such as doping heteroatom (Au, Ag, W, V, Mn, Pt) in semiconductor sonosensitizers to reduce bandgaps or constructing heterojunctions to inhibit e−–h+ pair recombination were exploited to enhance the ROS production of inorganic sonosensitizers [51–53]. Moreover, various researches have been reported that intratumoral O2 supplement could be improved by nanozymes with catalase (CAT)-like catalytic activity [54–56]. Therefore, developing multifunctional stimulate-responsive drug delivery nanoplatform with both sonodynamic performance and multiple enzyme-mimic catalytic activity is imperative to realize cascaded amplification of ROS production via collaborative chemo-sonodynamic-nanocatalytic cancer therapy.

CoSnO3 has drawn wide attention in the energy storage, electrocatalysis, and photocatalytic field due to the high theoretical capacity and adjustable valence state [57–60]. The narrow bandgap of CoSnO3-based nanomaterials is expected to endow them with excellent sonodynamic properties. Meanwhile, CoSnO3 might possess multiple enzyme-like catalytic activities due to the presence of mixed valences of Co and Sn ions. However, as far as we know, CoSnO3 has not been reported to be employed as nanozymes or sonosensitizers for NCT and SDT. In this work, we for the first time synthesized amorphous CoSnO3 nanocubes with efficient sonodynamic properties and triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities owing to their narrow bandgaps (1.6 eV) and the presence of Co2+ and Sn4+. To further improve the SDT and NCT performances of CoSnO3 nanocubes, we then constructed graphene quantum dot (GQD)/CoSnO3 Z-scheme heterojunctions by depositing GQD topoisomerases inhibitors on the surface of CoSnO3 nanocubes. The heterojunction-fabricated GQD/CoSnO3 could utilize the advantages of the two components based on the following new effects:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedure of DNA-major-groove-targeted GQDs was presented in Fig. 1 a, which revealed that the containing-nitrogen precursor julolidine was selected to synthesize GQDs through an ultrafast microwave reaction in the ethanol and oxalic acid solution. TEM image showed that the prepared GQDs possessed good dispersibility, exhibiting an average size of 3.6 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, the honeycombed lattice structure and 0.21 nm lattice spacing of GQDs were observed in their high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c), indicating the successful synthesis of high-quality GQDs. The high-quality single crystals of GQDs were also confirmed by their FFT image (inset in Fig. 1c), revealing a sixfold symmetrical FFT patterns. Raman spectrum also indicated that GQDs possessed few defects, where the intensity of ordered G band was higher than that of the disordered D band (Fig. S1). A broad peak at approximately 22° can be observed in the XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 1n), which corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [66]. XPS spectra were then performed to investigate the chemical composition of GQDs. As depicted in Fig. S2, GQDs were mainly composed of C, N, and O elements, in which the N content was up to 17.94 at%, suggesting that the highly N-doped characteristics of GQDs. Further analysis of the type of N-containing functional groups revealed the presence of pyridine N and graphitic N species (Fig. S2). The possible protonation of pyridine N could lead to a strong positive charge on the surface of GQDs, which was demonstrated by the zeta potential measurements (Fig. 1o). The as-prepared GQDs also exhibited excellent optical properties, indicating that the emission peak of GQDs was about 550 nm under 460 nm excitation (Fig. S3).

The synthesis of CoSnO3 nanocubes involved the following two steps. Firstly, we prepared CoSn(OH)6 nanocubes through the fast stoichiometric co-precipitation of SnCl4 and CoCl2 in the present of NaOH. Then, CoSnO3 nanocubes were obtained by dehydration of CoSn(OH)6 in N2 atmosphere. The nanocube structure of CoSnO3 was confirmed by their TEM image (Fig. 1d), suggesting that the size of the prepared CoSnO3 nanocubes was ∼ 130 nm. The size of nanocubes was also measured by DLS, indicating that the hydrodynamic diameter of CoSnO3 was 164.6 nm (Fig. S4a). No significant lattice fringe was observed in the high-resolution TEM image of CoSnO3 nanocubes, indicating that CoSnO3 nanocubes possessed an amorphous structure (Fig. S5). The amorphous structure of CoSnO3 nanocubes was also confirmed by their XRD pattern (Fig. 1n), which exhibited no obvious characteristic peaks associated with CoSn(OH)6 except for a broad diffraction peak at 34°. For comparison, the distinct diffraction peaks of CoSn(OH)6 nanocubes were detected in their XRD pattern, which were consistent with the standard pattern of perovskite-type CoSn(OH)6 (PDF#13–0356) [67]. XRD patterns clearly suggested the complete transformation of the crystal form of the prepared samples before and after annealing, verifying the successful preparation of amorphous CoSnO3 nanocubes. We also utilized XPS measurements to analyze the valence state of Co and Sn ions in the CoSnO3 nanocubes. The survey XPS spectrum of CoSnO3 nanocubes presented in Fig. S6a revealed the existence of Co, Sn, and O elements. Two satellite peaks at 781.2 and 796.3 eV were detected in the high-resolution Co 2p spectrum of CoSnO3 nanocubes, which were ascribed to Co2+ 2p3/2 and Co2+ 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. S6b). The existence of Sn4+ ions was also confirmed by the high-resolution Sn 3d spectrum of CoSnO3 nanocubes, showing two satellite peaks at 486.5 and 495.1 eV corresponded to Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. S6c). The zeta potential of CoSnO3 was determined to be −18.2 ± 0.7 mV (Fig. 1o).

We then assembled positively charged GQDs and negatively charged CoSnO3 nanocubes into GQD/CoSnO3 Z-scheme heterojunctions through electrostatic interactions. TEM image exhibited that a large number of GQDs were dispersed on the surface of CoSnO3 nanocubes (Fig. 1e). Compared with pristine CoSnO3, the size of the prepared heterojunctions was increased. Further high-resolution TEM image revealed that the distinct lattice spacing of 0.21 nm ascribed to GQDs were detected in the GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions, forcefully confirming the successful loading of GQDs on CoSnO3 nanocubes. The successful deposition of GQDs was also demonstrated by the XRD pattern of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions, which revealed two diffraction peaks located at approximately 22° and 34°, corresponding to GQDs and CoSnO3, respectively (Fig. 1n). Apart from XRD patterns, the absorption spectrum of GQD/CoSnO3 also indicated the successful preparation of heterojunctions, which exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at about 450 nm attributed to GQDs (Fig. 1g). After assembling, the zeta potential of CoSnO3 was reversed from −18.2 ± 0.7 mV to 7.8 ± 0.4 mV owing to the deposition of positively charged GQDs (Fig. 1o). We then utilized XPS characterization to investigate the changes in the valence states of Co and Sn ions in CoSnO3 nanocubes before and after GQD loading. In addition to Co, Sn, and O elements, the C and N elements also appeared in the survey XPS spectra of GQD/CoSnO3 (Fig. 1h), manifesting the successful introduction of N-doped GQDs. Unlike pristine CoSnO3 containing only Co2+ ions, the GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions contained both Co2+ and Co3+ (Fig. 1i), elucidating that the loading of GQDs could partially oxidize CoSnO3. The high-resolution C 1 s and N 1 s spectra of GQD/CoSnO3 showed the binding energies of C-C/C = C, C-N, C-OH, pyridine N, and graphitic N (Fig. 1k, l), which was consistent with that of GQDs (Fig. S2).

Upon verifying the successful construction of GQD/CoSnO3, we then evaluated the enhanced sonodynamic property of heterojunctions compared with single-component GQDs and CoSnO3 nanocubes by measuring their 1O2 generation efficiency upon US irradiation (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2, 50 % duty cycle). The absorption of DPBF showed the largest decrease in GQD/CoSnO3 group within 5 min period of US irradiation, revealing that the 1O2 generation ability of heterojunctions was higher than that of pure CoSnO3 nanocubes (Fig. 2 a, b). Meanwhile, GQDs could not decompose DPBF under US irradiation, indicated that GQDs has no sonodynamic activity (Fig. 2c). Then, the rate constant of 1O2 generation in GQD/CoSnO3 group was determined to be 0.41 min−1, which was 2.3 times than that of pure CoSnO3 (0.18 min−1) (Fig. 2d), demonstrating the superior sonodynamic effect of GQD/CoSnO3. In addition, we assessed the 1O2 generation efficiency of GQDs, CoSnO3, and GQD/CoSnO3 through the ESR spectroscopy using TEMP to trap 1O2. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the higher 1O2 signal was observed in GQD/CoSnO3 group compared with single CoSnO3, manifesting that more 1O2 were produced by GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions upon US stimulation.

To unveil the enhanced sonodynamic mechanism of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions, we constructed an energy band model by measuring the bandgap (E g), valence band potential (E VB), and conduction band potential (E CB) of single-component GQDs and CoSnO3. The bandgap of GQDs and CoSnO3 was determined to be 2.1 and 1.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 2f). Such narrow bandgap of CoSnO3 nanocubes (1.6 eV) makes it easy to be excited by US, exhibiting improved sonodynamic activity compared to traditional inorganic sonosensitizers represented by TiO2-based nanomaterials. Then, we investigated the E VB of GQDs and CoSnO3 through the XPS-VB spectra, which was 0.3 and 0.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 2g, h). The E CB of GQDs and CoSnO3 was then calculated to be −1.8 and −0.7 eV, respectively, based on the values of E VB and E g. Therefore, the CB and VB potential of GQDs located above that of CoSnO3 (Fig. 2i). To balance the Fermi level of single-component GQDs and CoSnO3 after the construction of the heterogeneous structure, an interfacial electric field will be formed between the heterogeneous interface of GQD and CoSnO3. Because GQDs and CoSnO3 were positively and negatively charged, respectively (Fig. 1o), the internal electric field within GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions pointed from GQDs to CoSnO3. Therefore, the US-excited electron would transfer from the CB of CoSnO3 to the VB of GQDs to form Z-scheme heterojunction, which inhibited the rapid recombination of electrons and holes in the single semiconductor. The Z-scheme heterojunction-fabricated GQD/CoSnO3 could exhibited enhanced 1O2 production efficiency owing to the more negative E CB potential for O2/O2 − redox potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heterojunction-enhanced triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities of GQD/CoSnO3
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the co-existence of Co2+, Co3+, and Sn4+, we envisioned that GQD/CoSnO3 possessed triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities. TMB, which could be oxidized to blue oxTMB in the presence of OH, was utilized to detect the POD-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a-c, the absorbance of TMB in GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3 groups at 652 nm increased significantly with increasing H2O2 concentration at acidic media (pH 4.5), while that in GQD group exhibit insignificant change. The increased TMB absorbance could be ascribed to the Fenton-like reaction mediated by Co2+ in an acidic environment. We then calculated the V max and K m values of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions and CoSnO3 nanocubes to quantitatively analyze their catalytic activity. Fig. 3d exhibited that heterojunctions possessed the higher V max (1.4 × 10−7 M s−1) and lower K m (1.2 mM) compared with pristine CoSnO3 nanocubes (1.1 × 10−7 M s−1 for V max and 1.4 mM for K m), suggesting the enhanced POD-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3 owing to the heterojunction-accelerated electron transfer process. The augmented POD-mimic catalytic activity of heterojunctions was also detected at pH 6.5 (Fig. 3e, f), showing a stronger TMB absorbance in GQD/CoSnO3 group compared with single-component CoSnO3 group. It also should be noted that Co2+-mediated Fenton-like reaction was more likely to occur under lower pH conditions, which revealed a lower K m and higher V max of GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3 at pH 4.5 compared with that at pH 6.5 (Fig. 3g, h). Fig. S7 showed that GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3 were unable to react with H2O2 to generation OH at pH 7.4. In addition, we also investigated the OH generation ability of GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3 in the absence of H2O2. As depicted in Fig. S8, TMB catalyzed by CoSnO3 or GQD/CoSnO3 alone revealed no obvious absorption peak at 655 nm, indicating that they have no ability of OH generation in the absence of H2O2. These results suggested that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions exhibited the pH-dependent and H2O2-dependent POD-mimic catalytic activity.

Apart from POD-mimic catalytic activity, we also investigated the CAT-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, and GQDs. Fig. 3i showed the comparison between GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, and GQD catalyzing H2O2 to produce oxygen molecules (O2), revealing that GQD/CoSnO3 possessed higher CAT-mimic catalytic activity compared with pristine CoSnO3. We then investigated the O2 generation efficiency of GQD/CoSnO3 and CoSnO3 with different H2O2 concentration (0–0.4 mM) at pH 4.5. As presented in Fig. 3j, k, GQD/CoSnO3 or CoSnO3 produced more O2 molecules at the higher H2O2 concentrations, manifesting their H2O2-concentration-dependent O2 generation behaviors. The higher O2 generation ability of heterojunctions was also demonstrated by the Michaelis-Menton kinetics analysis, exhibiting a higher V max (3.5 × 10−6 M s−1) and lower K m (0.1 mM) values compared with single CoSnO3 nanocubes (3.1 × 10−6 M s1 for V max and 0.3 mM for K m). In addition to H2O2-concentration-dependent O2 generation behaviors, GQD/CoSnO3 also exhibited pH-dependent CAT-mimic catalytic activity, revealing that heterojunctions could produce more O2 at the same H2O2 concentration under an acidic media compared to the normal physiological conditions (Fig. S9).

Apart from the presence of Co2+, the existence of high valence metal ions (Co3+ and Sn4+) that motivated us to investigate whether GQD/CoSnO3 possessed excellent GSH-px-mimic catalytic activity. A colorimetric reaction based on DTNB was utilized to assess the GSH depletion ability of GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3, and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3m, n, the characteristic absorption peak of GSH in GQD/CoSnO3 decreased more than that of CoSnO3, while no obvious GSH depletion ability of GQDs can be detected (Fig. 3o). Furthermore, the higher GSH depletion efficiency was observed in GQD/CoSnO3 group compared with CoSnO3 alone group (Fig. 3p). Considering that GSH would consume GQD/CoSnO3-mediated ROS production through POD-mimic catalytic reaction in the TME, we then compared the POD-mimic catalytic activity of GQD/CoSnO3 in the presence or absence of GSH. As depicted in Fig. S10, S11, more ROS was produced by GQD/CoSnO3 in the presence GSH at pH 4.5 and 6.5, which could be ascribed to the GSH-mediated transformation of Co3+ to Co2+. More Co2+ ions could further enhance the POD-mimic catalytic activity of heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive GQD release behavior of GQD/CoSnO3
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the expected pH-responsive GQD release, GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions should respond to the acidic TME. The biodegradable behavior of CoSnO3 and the pH-responsive release behavior of GQDs were investigated by dialysis of GQD/CoSnO3 in buffer solutions at pH 4.5, 6.5, or 7.4, which mimicked the normal physiological conditions and the acidic TME of tumor tissues. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the supernatant were monitored during the 7-day dialysis to assess their release rate. As depicted in Fig. 4 a and S12a, the absorbance of the supernatants at 458 nm, which was the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs, significantly increased after 3-day dialysis at pH 6.5 and 4.5, indicating the increased amount of free GQDs in supernatant solution. After measuring the absorption standard curve of GQDs, the released GQDs were determined to be about 23.2 ± 2.3 % and 14.8 ± 2.0 % during the 7-day dialysis at pH 4.5 and 6.5, respectively, (Fig. 4c), while GQDs in GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions were almost not released at pH 7.4. Apart from absorption spectrum, the fluorescence spectra of the supernatant also demonstrated a significant increase after dialysis of 3 days at pH 6.5 and 4.5 (Fig. 4b and S12b), suggesting the obvious GQD release from GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions at the acidic condition. It should be noted that the absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQD/CoSnO3 had no obvious change during the first two days in the acidic condition, indicating that the structure of heterojunctions remained stable, which might provide a stable window period for SDT and NCT. To straightforward observe the pH-responsive release behaviors of GQDs, the photographs of the supernatant under natural light and UV light were captured after dialysis of 0, 1, 3, and 7 days. Fig. S15 revealed that the color of supernatants changed from colorless to brown and the yellow fluorescence was gradually appeared after dialysis of 3 days at pH 4.5, elucidating that GQDs were released from heterojunctions at the acidic condition. On the contrary, no significant color changes of supernatants upon natural light or UV light were observed after dialysis of 7 days at pH 7.4 (Fig. S16), indicating that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions did not exhibit biodegradability under the normal physiological conditions. The pH-responsive biodegradable behaviors of GQD/CoSnO3 were further demonstrated by TEM images. At pH 6.5 and 4.5, some small fragments appeared after 3-day dialysis and more fragments were formed after 7-day dialysis (Fig. 4e-h and S13a-c), clearly confirming that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions with cubic morphology were successfully degraded into small fragments at the acidic condition. In contrast, a cube structure of heterojunctions was detected in the TEM image after 7-day dialysis at pH 7.4 (Fig. 4d). Collectively, these results demonstrated that CoSnO3 nanocubes could degrade after 3 days under the acidic conditions, thereby releasing GQDs from GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions.

Considering that GQD/CoSnO3 would not degrade within 2 days, we then investigated the stable window period of heterojunctions for enhanced SDT by measuring their sonodynamic properties at different times under the acidic (pH 6.5 and 4.5) or neutral condition (pH 7.4). At pH 7.4 (Fig. S17), the 1O2 generation efficiency of GQD/CoSnO3 after 1-day or 7-day dialysis was similar to that of 0-day (Fig. 2a). Under the acidic condition (pH 6.5 and 4.5), the sonodynamic properties of GQD/CoSnO3 after dialysis of 1 day exhibited insignificant decrease compared with that of pristine GQD/CoSnO3 (Fig. 4i and S14a), suggesting that heterojunctions would not degrade within 1 day. However, the structure of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions was slowly destroyed after 3 days at pH 6.5 and 4.5 owing to the pH-responsive biodegradable behavior, resulting in a continuous weakening of the SDT effect (Fig. 4j, k and S14b, c). Specifically, the rate constant of 1O2 generation in GQD/CoSnO3 group at pH 6.5 was markedly reduced from the initial 0.42 min−1 to 0.12 and 0.07 min−1 after 3-day and 7-day dialysis, respectively (Fig. 4l). These results clearly confirmed that the US-activated ROS generation function via GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions could be completed in a stable 1-day window period.

We next evaluated the pH-responsive GQD release behaviors at the cellular level. Based on the fluorescence of GQD itself, the released GQDs in 143B cells after incubation of different times was monitored using confocal microscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 4m, no significant green fluorescence represented GQDs can be observed in 143B cells after incubation of 2 days, indicating that GQDs were not released from heterojunctions. After 3-day incubation of GQD/CoSnO3, a weak green fluorescence appeared in the cytoplasm of 143B cells. As the incubation time extended to 5 or 7 days, the green fluorescence signal in 143B cells increased, illustrating the large appearance of free GQDs within the cells. Notably, the confocal images also clearly showed that GQDs could enter the nucleus of 143B cells, and its fluorescence intensity in the nucleus was significantly stronger than that in the cytoplasm. The phenomenon was consistent with our previous report regarding of GQDs,[65] which could bind to the major groove of DNA, inhibit topoisomerase, induce DNA damage, and lead to apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antitumor effects of GQD/CoSnO3
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent sonodynamic properties and triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities of GQD/CoSnO3 as well as the pH-responsive release behaviors of GQDs, we then investigated the synergistic effect of chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT at the cellular level. The cellular uptake of GQD/CoSnO3 was initially investigated using confocal microscope. ICG was used as a NIR fluorescent probe to label GQD/CoSnO3. Bright NIR fluorescence in the cytoplasm was observed in the confocal images (Fig. S18), suggesting efficient cellular internalization of GQD/CoSnO3. Fig. S19 revealed that CoSnO3 alone and GQD/CoSnO3 both exhibited insignificantly cytotoxic to human normal liver LO2 cells after incubating for 48 h even at a concentration of 400 μg/mL, suggesting their excellent biocompatibility. In contrast, a certain cytotoxic effect of CoSnO3 and GQD/CoSnO3 against 143B cells were observed in Fig. 5 a and S20b, attributing to the production of OH in the tumor cells with higher H2O2 concentration in comparison with normal cells. Notably, the cytotoxicity effect of GQD/CoSnO3 was higher than that of CoSnO3, which could be ascribed to the higher POD-mimic catalytic activity of heterojunctions. For free GQDs, a dose-dependent cytotoxicity was detected in 143B cells after incubation of 24 or 48 h (Fig. S20a), which was consistent with our previous report.[65] It should be noted that no cytotoxicity against LO2 cells treated with GQD/CoSnO3 for 48 h was detected, suggesting that GQDs were not released from heterojunctions in the normal cells. Considering the pH-responsive GQD release behaviors of GQD/CoSnO3, the in vitro cytotoxicity effect of GQD/CoSnO3 against 143B cells was further investigated by prolonging the incubation time to 72 and 96 h. Fig. 5b revealed that the viability of 143B cells incubated with GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions decreased continuously with the increase of the incubation time. After incubation of GQD/CoSnO3 (400 μg/mL) for 96 h, the viability of 143B cells was attenuated to 40.3 %, manifesting that GQDs in GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions were released and then induced apoptosis to enhance the therapeutic effect of NCT. These above results indicated that GQD/CoSnO3 could take advantage of the TME to achieve specific decomposition and pH-responsive GQD release behaviors. In addition, CoSnO3 might be used as a carrier of chemotherapeutic drug to effectively avoid the biological toxicity of chemotherapy. We also evaluated the in vitro therapeutic effect of combined NCT and SDT via GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions. Fig. 5c exhibited that the viability of 143B cells treated with GQD/CoSnO3 for 24 h under US irradiation decreased to about 20 %, which was much lower than that of GQD/CoSnO3 without US irradiation (Fig. 5b), indicating the synergetic therapeutic effect of SDT and NCT. Moreover, the cytotoxicity of 143B cells treated with GQD/CoSnO3 was higher than that of pristine CoSnO3 at the same condition (Fig. S21), demonstrating the heterojunction-enhanced sonodynamic properties and triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activities. After prolonging the incubation time to 72 or even 96 h, 143B cells incubated with GQD/CoSnO3 almost died (Fig. 5d), which could be attributed to the chemotherapeutic effect of the released GQDs as well as the combined NCT-SDT effect via GQD/CoSnO3.

In addition to MTT assay, we also utilized live/dead cell staining to investigate the synergetic therapeutic effect of chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT via GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions. A slight red fluorescence represented dead cells was detected in GQD/CoSnO3 alone group (Fig. 5e), illustrating that heterojunctions could induce cell damage through POD-mimic catalytic reaction. Moreover, the stronger red fluorescence was detected in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group, elucidating that the heterojunction-mediated SDT and NCT possessed higher antitumor efficacy compared with single NCT. More importantly, the green fluorescence was not observed after the incubation time of GQD/CoSnO3 with 143B cells extending to 3 days, suggesting that GQDs could be released after the degradation of heterojunctions. For ROS staining, no green fluorescence signal can be detected in the control and US alone group, indicating no significant ROS generation (Fig. 5f). In contrast, a slight green fluorescence was appeared in GQD/CoSnO3 group, suggesting that heterojunction-mediated Fenton-like reaction could generate ROS. The stronger ROS signal was observed in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group, demonstrating that a large amount of ROS was generated by GQD/CoSnO3 through heterojunction-enhanced SDT and NCT. After the incubation time extending to 72 h, the green fluorescence was also detected in the cytoplasm of 143B cell treated with GQD/CoSnO3 under US irradiation. In addition, the mitochondrial membrane potential detection kit (JC-1) was applied to determine the disruption effect of GQD/CoSnO3 in mitochondria. As shown in Fig. S22, the strongest green fluorescence was detected in the GQD/CoSnO3 + US group, verifying the highest ROS level. Moreover, the JC-1 aggregate to monomer ratio in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group was lower than that in the other groups (Fig. S23), indicating that GQD/CoSnO3 significantly decreased the mitochondrial membrane potential. These above results demonstrated that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions could integrate several therapeutic modalities of chemotherapy, NCT, and SDT into one platform to achieve satisfying anticancer efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor effects of GQD/CoSnO3
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent results obtained in the in vitro experiments, the in vivo combined chemotherapy, NCT, and SDT via GQD/CoSnO3 were investigated by using 143B-tumor-bearing mice. We first used NIR imaging to investigate the passive targeting ability of GQD/CoSnO3. As illustrated in Fig. 6 b, d, after intravenous injection of ICG-labeled GQD/CoSnO3, the gradually increased fluorescence could be observed. The tumor accumulation ability of GQD/CoSnO3 was also confirmed by the ex vivo NIR fluorescent imaging of major organs and tumor in mice, which exhibited that the highest accumulation amount in tumor tissues can be detected at 24 h post-injection (Fig. 6c, e). The accumulation of GQD/CoSnO3 in tumor tissue was further demonstrated by ICP-MS, which was performed to measure the Ti content after injecting of heterojunctions for 24 h. Fig. 6f exhibited that GQD/CoSnO3 mainly accumulated in tumor and liver. Based on these results, the optimum time point of US irradiation was 24 h post-injection of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions (Fig. 6a).

We then explored the antitumor efficacy of GQD/CoSnO3-mediated chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT. By comparison with the control and US alone groups, the tumor volumes in GQD/CoSnO3 alone group were partially inhibited (Fig. 6g), indicating a certain antitumor effect of GQD/CoSnO3-induced synergistic chemotherapy and NCT. Moreover, the single-component CoSnO3 nanocubes under US irradiation exhibited a certain inhibitory effect on tumor growth, suggesting the CoSnO3-mediated SDT could not achieve satisfactory therapeutic effect. Notably, the tumors in GQD/CoSnO3 plus US irradiation group revealed the complete eradication during the 14-day treatment period (Fig. S24), which could be ascribed to the enhanced SDT and NCT efficacy of heterojunctions as well as the TME-responsive GQD release for inducing continuous chemotherapy. We also monitored the survival time of 143B tumor-bearing mice after different treatments to investigate their antitumor efficacy. As illustrated in Fig. 6h, the mice in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group were all alive after 50 days, demonstrating that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions possesses inhibitory effects on tumor recurrence. In addition, GQD/CoSnO3 in the presence of US irradiation exhibited no pulmonary metastatic nodule after 14 days, while a considerable number of pulmonary metastatic nodules can be observed in the other groups (Fig. S25). To further reveal the excellent synergistic chemo-SDT-NCT efficacy of GQD/CoSnO3, mice in each treatment group were euthanized after 24 h of US treatments and their tumors were collected for H&E and TUNEL staining. Fig. 6j, k revealed that the most severe tumor damage was observed in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group. Moreover, the ROS staining of tumor tissues suggested that the highest ROS signal was detected in GQD/CoSnO3 + US group compared with the other groups (Fig. S26), verifying the presence of heterojunctions could amplify the ROS generation of CoSnO3. These above results forcefully confirmed that the construction of pH-responsive heterojunctions could not only improve the SDT and NCT efficacy of single-component CoSnO3 nanocubes, but also achieve continuous chemotherapy through the release of GQDs.

Finally, the in vivo biocompatibility of GQD/CoSnO3-induced synergistic chemotherapy, NCT, and SDT was assessed through measuring the change of mice weight, detecting hematological tests, and performing histological analysis. During the 14-day treatment period, the body weight of mice in each treatment group remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 6i). Fig. S27 and S28 showed that the blood indexes of the mice in all groups were within a normal range, and no obvious lesions were observed in the major organs, indicating that GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions possessed good biosafety in vivo.

In summary, we fabricated the GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions by depositing GQD topoisomerase inhibitors on pH-responsive biodegradable CoSnO3 nanocubes. The formation of heterojunctions inhibits electron-hole recombination within GQD/CoSnO3 under US irradiation for enhanced sonodynamic activity, exhibiting higher rate constant of 1O2 generation (0.41 min−1), which is 2.3 times than that of pristine CoSnO3 nanocubes (0.18 min−1). The obtained GQD/CoSnO3 nanozymes could mimic POD, CAT, and GSH-px to realize the cascaded amplification of ROS generation through Co2+-mediated Fenton-like reaction, catalytic decomposition of endogenous H2O2 for relieving hypoxia, and Co3+/Sn4+-mediated depletion of overexpressed GSH. The pH-responsive biodegradable features of GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions allow GQDs to be specifically released in tumor tissues, thereby exerting chemotherapy effects and further enhancing SDT and NCT. Collectively, the satisfactory therapeutic effects including complete tumor eradication, complete suppression of metastasis, and survival prolongation, were achieved by GQD/CoSnO3 heterojunctions, which could be ascribed to the heterojunction-amplified sonodynamic properties and triple enzyme-mimic catalytic activity as well as the TME-responsive GQD release.

We employed a microwave-assisted solvothermal approach to synthesize N-doped GQDs using julolidine and oxalic acid. Specifically, 0.025 g of julolidine was dissolved in a 10 mL ethanol solution and 0.4 mL oxalic acid. Then, the mixed solution was heated at 200 °C for 10 min. After the microwave reaction, the obtained GQDs were purified by ultrasonic extraction with petroleum ether for several times.

5 mL SnCl4·6H2O and 1 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O were mixed in 30 mL aqueous solution. 5 mL of NaOH solution (2 M) was added to the mixture solution followed by stirring for 1 h. Then, 20 mL of NaOH solution (8 M) was slowly added to the mixed solution followed by stirring for 15 min. Finally, CoSn(OH)6 was subjected to annealing at different temperatures (150, 300, and 500 ℃) for 2 h under N2 atmosphere protection to obtain CoSnO3 nanocubes.
10 mL of GQDs (1 mg/mL) were mixed with 1 mL of CoSnO3 nanocubes (1 mg/mL) followed by stirring for 12 h. After stirring, the unloaded GQDs were removed through centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min.

TEM images of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 were obtained using JEM-2100F microscope. XRD patterns and XPS analyses were performed by Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 and Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometers, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 were analyzed using Hitachi 3100 spectrophotometers. ESR spectra of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 were recorded using a Bruker EMXplus ESR spectrometer.

60 μL of DPBF was added to 3 mL of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 (400 μg/mL). After US irradiation, the absorption spectra of the mixture solution were measured every minutes. For ESR measurements, CoSnO3, GQDs, or GQD/CoSnO3 solution was mixed with 20 µL of TEMP and then exposed to US irradiation for 2 min. ESR spectrum was then carried out to determine the generation of 1O2.

300 μL of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 at the same concentration of 400 μg/mL were mixed with H2O2 with varied concentrations (0–2.0 mM) in different buffer (pH 4.5, 6.5 or 7.4) containing TMB (50 mM, 10 μL). Then, absorption spectra were performed to measure the absorbance change of the mixture solution. Finally, the POD-mimic catalytic activities of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 were analyzed by Michaelis-Menten fitting.

A portable dissolved oxygen meter (JPBJ-607) was utilized to detect the O2 molecule concentrations in the mixture solution of GQD/CoSnO3 (400 μg/mL) and H2O2 (0, 50, 100, 200, 400 μM) in different buffer (pH 4.5, 6.5 or 7.4). For comparison, the O2 production ability of CoSnO3 and GQDs was also measured at the same conditions. Finally, the CAT-mimic catalytic activities of CoSnO3 and GQDs were analyzed by Michaelis-Menten fitting.

1 mM GSH was initially mixed with 3 mL of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3 at the same concentrations (400 μg/mL). After incubation for different times, 100 μL of mixture solution was added in 900 μL of PBS solution containing 2 μL DTNB. The absorption spectrum of the mixture solution was measured to determine the GSH-px-mimic catalytic activity of CoSnO3, GQDs, and GQD/CoSnO3.

To investigate the pH-responsive GQD release behaviors, GQD/CoSnO3 were dialyzed in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.5) or PBS solution (pH 7.4) for 7 days. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the supernatant were measured at different times to determine the pH-responsive release behaviors. GQD/CoSnO3 in different buffer solution was collected at 0, 1, 3, and 7 days for TEM characterization. The sonodynamic performance evaluation of GQD/CoSnO3 after dialysis of different times was also performed.

LO2 and 143B cells were purchased from Cell Bank, Chinese Academy of Sciences. For the biocompatibility evaluation, CoSnO3, GQDs, or GQD/CoSnO3 with varied concentrations (0–400 µg/mL) was incubated with LO2 cells for 24 or 48 h. Based on the provided protocols, a standard MTT assay was performed to evaluate the biocompatibility. For in vitro combined chemotherapy, SDT, and NCT, CoSnO3, GQDs, or GQD/CoSnO3 was incubated with 143B cells for 4 h and the US irradiation was carried out for 5 min. 143B cells were then incubated for another 24, 48, 72, or 96 h followed by a standard MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead cell staining and ROS staining
ce:section.ce:para: For live/dead cell staining, calcein AM and PI were utilized to stain 143B cells after different treatments including control, US, GQD/CoSnO3, GQD/CoSnO3 + US (24 h), and GQD/CoSnO3 + US (72 h). The fluorescence images were captured using Olympus fluorescence microscope. For in vitro ROS detection, DCFH-DA was utilized as a probe to evaluate the intracellular ROS production of 143B cells after different treatments. The ROS staining images in each group were obtained by Olympus confocal microscope.

The mitochondrial membrane potential detection kit (JC-1) was applied to determine the disruption effect of GQD/CoSnO3 in mitochondria. 143B cells were incubated in confocal dishes for 24 h. After different treatments (Control, US, GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3 + US, or GQD/CoSnO3 + US), 143B cells were washed with PBS and incubated in JC-1 staining solution at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, fluorescent images were captured by a confocal microscope.

All animal experiments were carried out under the permission by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University (SYXK 2019–0020). Five-week-old female Balb/c nude mice were obtained from Shanghai SLAC Laboratory Animal Co. Ltd. After intravenous injection of ICG-labeled GQD/CoSnO3 (400 μg/mL), the NIR fluorescence images of mice were captured by IVIS Lumina III in vivo imaging system at different times.

After the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3, 143B tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups (n = 5) as follows: control, US, GQD/CoSnO3, CoSnO3 + US, GQD/CoSnO3 + US. US treatments (50 kHz, 1.0 W/cm2, 50 % duty cycle) were performed after intravenous injection of CoSnO3 or GQD/CoSnO3 for 24 h. During the treatment period, mice in each group were regularly monitored to determine the body weight and tumor volume. After 2-day treatment, tumors of mice in each group were collected for H&E and TUNEL staining. After 14-day treatment, major organs and tumors were harvested for histopathological analysis and hematological tests.

All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). Statistical differences were analyzed using a Student's t-test. * denotes a statistical significance (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001) between the experimental data of two groups.
